meta.roomSetup: Projector + presenter device; learners on their own devices via the Welcome-slide QR; tables of 3-6 for group activities.
meta.futureUse: The facilitator version of the site will mirror the learner deck with these notes rendered in a rail to the right of each slide. Keys map to slide section ids.
meta.postSession: Two weeks out, send one line to attendees (or their managers): 'Did your first rep happen? Reply with one sentence.' That reply is your Kirkpatrick Level 3 evidence; collect and tally.

full.description: Everything as designed: all activities, the in-class video hook, pair shares. Book a 90-minute slot; the sum includes realistic transitions inside each block.

core.description: The 60-minute slot: every simulator, every check, the Lab, the capstone, and ONE group activity of the facilitator's choice; other group activities and videos become follow-ups. Per-section cuts are in each coreNote.

sections.title: Welcome / QR
sections.purpose: Get every learner into the experience on their own device before you start.
facilitate: Have this slide projected as people arrive.
Say: 'Scan the code — everything we do today is hands-on, and this puts the controls in your hands.'
Confirm by show of hands that most of the room has the site open before advancing.
sections.validate: 80%+ of the room has the deck open on a device.
sections.watchFor: Anyone without a device — pair them with a neighbor now so the later solo activities still work.
sections.coreNote: Core: one scan prompt, move on.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.purpose: Set the contract: five objectives, no code, no math.
facilitate: Read the five objectives aloud; they are the assessment map (quiz validates 1-4, the capstone plan validates 5).
Say: 'If any of these is the one you came for, tell me at the break and I'll go deeper there.'
sections.validate: Objectives acknowledged; note which one the room cares most about.
sections.watchFor: Anxiety about being 'behind' on AI — name it: this session assumes zero background.

sections.title: The plan
sections.purpose: Orient: nine ideas in order, ending with wellbeing — not just capability.
facilitate: Point out 09: 'We end with what AI does to your workload, not just what it can do.'
sections.coreNote: Core: skip; the progress bar orients.

sections.title: 01 · What AI is (rings simulator)
sections.purpose: Replace the fuzzy word 'AI' with a four-layer mental model.
facilitate: Tap each ring outward-in on the projector; learners follow on their devices.
Run the AS A GROUP activity (5 min): teams list daily tools, sort them into layers, report their top three.
Debrief one table's list; correct any tool mis-sorted with curiosity, not correction.
sections.validate: Inline quick check on the slide (generative-AI ring question) — watch the room's first-click accuracy; re-teach if wrong answers dominate.
sections.watchFor: People equating AI with only ChatGPT — the rings exist to break exactly that.
sections.coreNote: Core: rings + check; skip the group sort.

sections.title: 02 · How machines learn (ML trainer game)
sections.purpose: The three training paradigms, anchored to workplace data.
facilitate: Optional in-flow video: play the first 2 minutes of 'How Machines Learn' (embedded on the slide) as a hook; assign the rest as homework.
Walk the three cards, then say: 'Now you be the ML engineer.'
Run the PLAY widget (4 scenarios) together on the projector — vote by hands for each scenario before revealing.
Run the AS A GROUP activity: teams pick a dataset their unit already produces and match a learning type to it.
sections.validate: ML trainer score visible per learner (3/4+ = got it); group share-out names a plausible dataset + type pairing.
sections.watchFor: The supervised/unsupervised distinction is the common stumble — the tell is 'were there labels?'
sections.coreNote: Core: trainer with hand-votes; assign video and group match as follow-ups.

sections.title: 03 · Neural networks (run-the-network)
sections.purpose: Demystify 'the black box': learning = weight adjustment, which is why explainability is hard.
facilitate: Press Run on the projector; narrate each layer as it lights.
Run the AS A GROUP discussion on explainability (vendor question).
Collect one vendor question per table — these are gold for your AI-procurement conversations.
sections.validate: Inline quick check (what changes during training) + each table produces a vendor question.
sections.watchFor: Don't let the math-curious drag the room into gradient descent. The 3Blue1Brown series link now lives in this section's Go deeper for exactly them.
sections.coreNote: Core: run the network + check; skip the vendor discussion.

sections.title: 04 · Language models (token predictor)
sections.purpose: One idea: it predicts the next word. Everything else follows.
facilitate: Build the sentence live on the projector — pick the low-probability word once ('banana') for the laugh and the lesson.
Run the ON YOUR OWN activity: each person writes their tempted-to-trust task and its 30-second check. Tell them to keep the note for Section 07.
Close the section with the embedded 8-minute 3Blue1Brown LLM video if time allows, or assign it — it consolidates the token predictor exercise.
sections.validate: Ask 2-3 volunteers to read their task + check aloud; recap-quiz question 2 covers the concept.
sections.watchFor: 'So it's just autocomplete?' — yes, and: scale makes prediction surprisingly capable. Both halves are true.
sections.coreNote: Core: keep the solo note; it feeds Section 07.

sections.title: 05 · Temperature (slider)
sections.purpose: Why identical prompts give different answers — and how to steer it with words.
facilitate: Drag the slider live from Precise to Creative; read both extremes aloud.
Run the ON YOUR OWN activity (in-experience): learners place the slider for their own work task, read both extremes, and write the steering words for each.
Point at the 'later today' line: the real two-chat experiment happens after the Toolkit slide introduces the tools.
sections.validate: Inline quick check (policy summary = low temperature); volunteers describe what differed between their two answers.
sections.watchFor: Clarify most tools hide the dial — the words 'standard answer' vs 'ten wild options' ARE the dial.

sections.title: 06 · Prompting (anatomy + Persona Pattern)
sections.purpose: The four-part prompt; credit Vanderbilt's Dr. Jules White for the Persona Pattern.
facilitate: Hover each part of the example prompt on the projector.
Name-drop deliberately: 'The role line is the Persona Pattern — invented and taught by our own Dr. Jules White. His course is the featured next step on the closing slide.'
Run the ON YOUR OWN activity (5 min): a real email, four parts, two refinements. Neighbor screen-swap if time allows.
sections.validate: Screen-swap peer review; ask for one 'move I'm stealing' from the room. Recap questions 5 covers structure.
sections.watchFor: Perfectionists writing one giant prompt — push iteration: 'correct it like you'd coach a new hire.'
sections.coreNote: Core: 3-minute draft instead of 5; skip screen-swap.

sections.title: Prompt Lab (simulator: task → choices → outcome)
sections.purpose: Learners feel the outcome quality change as each prompt part improves — cause and effect, before writing their own.
facilitate: Frame the task aloud: 'Same announcement email — but this time the machine grades your build.'
Run it once on the projector choosing the WEAK options deliberately; read the greeting-card output for the laugh.
Everyone rebuilds on their own device aiming for 11+; on your own, ~4 min.
Debrief: which single upgrade moved your score most? (It's almost always Context.)
sections.validate: Learners reach 11-12/12 within two runs; ask for a show of hands. The per-slot coaching is the reteach.
sections.watchFor: Anyone gaming it by picking all C's without reading — ask them to say WHY the strong context wins.
sections.coreNote: Core: one projector build + one personal build.

sections.title: 07 · Strengths & limits (hallucination)
sections.purpose: Calibrated trust: brilliant drafter, unreliable librarian.
facilitate: Walk both columns; define hallucination with the key-term card.
Run the inline check (policy citation) together.
Run the AS A GROUP activity: tables surface their Section-04 notes, pick the highest-stakes one, assign the check and an owner.
sections.validate: Inline check + each table states task/check/owner aloud. This is the session's core transfer moment — don't rush it.
sections.watchFor: Cynics ('so it's useless') and enthusiasts ('it's always right') — the answer to both is the verification habit.
sections.coreNote: Core: keep; this is the transfer spine.

sections.title: 08 · Responsible use
sections.purpose: Bias, privacy, accountability — as team norms, not abstractions.
facilitate: Run the inline privacy check first — it's a safe failure that opens the conversation.
Run the AS A GROUP activity (7 min): draft the one-sentence team policy; stress-test it against the three cards.
Ask two tables to read their sentence; note convergence.
sections.validate: Every table produces a policy sentence and names who takes it to their team meeting.
sections.watchFor: Questions about specific Vanderbilt policy — point to official university AI guidance rather than improvising.
sections.coreNote: Core: 4-minute policy sentence; one table reads theirs.

sections.title: Your toolkit (Amplify / Copilot / ChatGPT + data levels)
sections.purpose: Route every task to the right platform, gated by data sensitivity. This is the slide that prevents incidents.
facilitate: Walk the three platforms with a one-line scenario each: internal draft → Amplify; email cleanup in Outlook → Copilot; public-info brainstorm → ChatGPT.
Acknowledge the wider world honestly: Claude, Perplexity, Gamma are good tools — green-level content only, because they sit outside VU agreements.
Read the RED list slowly, verbatim. Say: 'If it's on this list, it never touches a generative AI tool. Not once, not partially, not anonymized-ish.'
Run the inline check (staffing-plan question) as a room vote before revealing.
sections.validate: Inline check first-click accuracy; then cold-call one scenario per table: 'donor thank-you letter — which tool?'
sections.watchFor: 'But I already pasted X into ChatGPT once' confessions — respond with the fix (report per policy, change practice), not shame. Point to VU AI Guidance + Data Classification Policy.
sections.coreNote: Core: keep; read the red list verbatim regardless.

sections.title: 09 · Wellbeing (keeping the time AI saves)
sections.purpose: AI intensifies work by default; managers decide whether savings become breathing room. EAP is the safety net.
facilitate: Present the three patterns; cite the HBR research.
Read the 'If work feels heavier, say so' card verbatim — the EAP mention matters and should be said out loud.
Run the ON YOUR OWN self-check. Say explicitly: 'This one is private. Nothing is shared.'
sections.validate: Do NOT collect answers — validation here is the recap-quiz wellbeing question only. Privacy is the point.
sections.watchFor: This section can surface real strain. If someone discloses distress, connect them to EAP resources after the session — never process it in the room.
sections.coreNote: Core: patterns + EAP card; self-check as homework.

sections.title: Recap quiz
sections.purpose: Validate the four objectives with six scored questions.
facilitate: Everyone takes it on their own device; you take it on the projector last.
Ask for a show of hands: 5+ correct. Revisit the most-missed question live.
sections.validate: Quiz score = the session's learning evidence. 5/6 room-wide is the pass signal; below that, reteach the weakest section using its simulator.
sections.watchFor: Keep it light — this validates the teaching, not the learner.

sections.title: My AI plan (capstone builder)
sections.purpose: Convert the whole session into one personal, committed plan: real task, right tool by data level, starter prompt, verification check, and where the saved hour goes.
facilitate: Frame it: 'Everything today was practice. This is the real thing: one task, one tool, one plan.'
Build one on the projector with a volunteer's task so the room sees the flow (60 seconds).
Everyone builds their own, on their own, ~4 min. Remind them nothing is saved or sent; it lives on their screen.
Have them hit Copy and paste the plan somewhere they will see Monday: notes app, email to self, Teams.
Pairs: read your first line to your neighbor. 90 seconds.
If someone picks a red-level task, that is a win, not a failure: the plan tells them to stop. Praise it out loud.
sections.validate: Scan the room for plan cards on screens; show of hands for 'copied and pasted it somewhere real.' The plan IS the session's performance assessment.
sections.watchFor: Vague tasks ('email') produce vague plans. Push for one specific recurring artifact: 'the Thursday status note to my director.'
sections.coreNote: Core: keep whole; this is the payoff.

sections.title: Glossary
sections.purpose: Reference, not teaching. Flip two or three live.
facilitate: Skip live, or flip 'hallucination' as a 20-second callback. This is reference for later, not teaching.

sections.title: Closing
sections.purpose: Land the plane with ONE committed next step: Jules White's course, enrolled before they leave the page.
facilitate: Point at the featured card: 'Free to audit, taught by Vanderbilt faculty, ten times deeper on today's Section 06.'
Ask people to open it and enroll now if they mean it. Watching them do it beats hoping they will.
Close with one ask: 'Tell me one thing you'll do differently this week.' Take three answers and end.
Kirkpatrick L1, fist-to-five: 'Was this worth your hour? Zero to five fingers up, right now.' Note the room's number in your own log.
sections.validate: Show of hands: who enrolled or bookmarked? Fist-to-five reaction score. Three spoken commitments.
